images.caption: The looming presence of the BC Penitentiary dominated New Westminster for over a century until its closure in 1980.
One of the four ranges of the "Penthouse" in the BC Penitentiary, showing the elevated catwalk along which armed guards monitored prisoners, while they exercised outside their cells.

Segregation cell in the "Penthouse" after the "improvements" made following the Federal Court decision in the McCann case.

Before the improvements prisoners slept on the floor with only a foam pad.
The view from inside the "improved" segregation cell in the "Penthouse" looking out though the door.
The "Window of Contempt".
One of the showers in the "Penthouse".
The segregation exercise yard built after the McCann decision to provide prisoners their daily one hour of open air exercise time.
The oficial closing ceremony of the BC Penitentiary in 1980.

The legal team and the plaintiffs in the McCann case. From left to right: Jake Quiring, Ralph Cockrane, Bryan Williams (later Chief Justice of the BC Supreme Court), Michael Jackson, Mel Miller, Jack McCann, Don Sorochan and John Rubenstein. Not shown are the plaintiffs Andy Bruce, Walter Dudoward and Donny Oag.
A standard general population cell at Kent Institution.
The segregation exercise yard at Kent Institution. Prisoners are allowed one hour exercise a day.
The interview room in the segregation unit at Kent Institution. The prisoners' chair is bolted to the floor for security reasons. This is also the room in which many segregation reviews take place.
One of the ranges in J Unit, the segregation unit at Kent Institution.
The screened visit area at Kent Institution. Prisoners who are not cleared for open visits are required to talk to their visitors though a plexiglass screen by telephone.

The open visiting area at Kent Institution where prisoners are allowed to have physical face to face contact with their visitors. In Canadian penitentiaries most visits are open visits, except in the Special Handling Unit where all visits are closed screened visits. The visiting regime is descibed Sector 5, Chapter 3.
Main corridor, Kent Institution.
Prisoners' telephones new and old, Kent Institution.
Segregation cell, Kent Institution.
Segregation exercise yard, Kent Institution.
Segregation lunch, Kent Institution.
Shower, Segregation Unit, Kent Institution.
Double-bunked cell, Kent Institution.
The open visiting area, Kent Institution. The square box mounted in the middle of the table contains a microphone to enable staff to monitor prisoners' conversations.
A personal note in Hughie McDonald's cell.
Hughie McDonald's view of inside out.
The perimeter fence at Matsqui Institution.
The segregation exercise yard at Matsqui Institution.
Segregation cell at Matsqui Institution.

Jeff Blackburn looking out through the food slot in a segregation cell at Matsqui Institution. At the time this picture was taken in July 2001, Mr. Blackburn had spent 30 days in segregation. Ultimately he spent 74 days in the "hole".
Cell doors in the segregation unit at Matsqui Institution.
One of the guard towers at Matsqui Institution. These days, with the advent of electronic surveillance the towers are not utilised except in emergency situations.
Detail of a standard cell, Matsqui Institution.
Perimeter fence, Matsqui Institution.
Perimeter fence and main cell block, Matsqui Institution.
Segregation cell, Matsqui Institution.
Segregation Unit, Matsqui Institution.
Bill Fredrick whose experiences in segregation at Kent Institution in 1983 are set out in Sector 4, Chapter 1 of Justice behind the Walls. The picture was taken at Renous Institution in 1987.

Gary Weaver (right) and Bobo Dere at Millhaven Institution in 1992.

Mr. Weavers experiences with imprisonment are the subject of Sector 5, chapter 5 of Justice behind the Walls.

Gary Weaver and his "Bros" at Kent Institution in 1996. In the back row Kenny Makichuk is on the far left and Hughie McDonald is second from the right. In the front row Gary Weaver is second from the left and Neil Simpson is on the far right.
Gary Weaver, with his friend Robin, at a visit in William Head Institution in 2001.

Glen Rosenthal at Matsqui Institution in 2001.

Mr. Rosenthal's experience in segregation in Edmonton Institution and Kent Institution are described in Sector 4, Chapters 4 and 5 of Justice behind the Walls.

Greg Hanson, photgraphed at Matsqui Institution in 2002.

Mr. Hanson's experiences at Matsqui in Operation Big Scoop are described in Sector 2, Chapter 3, and his later experiences at Kent during an exceptional search are set out in Sector 5, Chapter 4 of Justice behind the Walls.

Hughie McDonald in Matsqui Institution in 2001.

Mr. McDonald's experiences at Kent Institution in 1994-96 are descibed in Sector 2, Chapter 4 of Justice behind the Walls.

John Conroy, Q.C. one of the legal pioneers in the field of prisoners rights, who has argued some of the leading cases in this area.

Mr. Conroy's efforts on behalf of prisoners appear thoughout Justice behind the Walls.
Michael Jackson and John Conroy, co-counsel in Winters v. Legal Services Society of B.C. in the Supreme Court of Canada.

Keith Jago at Kent Institution in 2001.

Mr. Jago's experieces at Kent in 1994 are described in Sector 3, Chapter 2 of Justice behind the Walls.
Keith Routley, the Senior Independant Chairperson presiding at the Matsqui Disiplinary Court.

Kevin Derochie sent me this photograph in 2001 after he was sent back to the Special Handling Unit in Quebec. On the back of the photo Mr. Deroches wrote:

"This is me a couple of months after I got out of prison after a 10 month seg trip. The psychological damage was so severe that the only time I felt good was after a blast. I'd never done a needle in my life, 33 years of it and suddenly I'm holing myself up in motel rooms doing an oz. a day and a gram of heroin."

Mr. Derochie's experieces in the SHU are described in Sector 5, Chapter 2 of Justice behind the Walls.
Kevin Derochie in the Special Handling Unit in 2001.

Mark Noon-Ward, photographed at Pacific Regional Headquarters in 2002.

Mr. Noon-Ward is a correctional officer at Kent Institution and leader of the Emergency Responce Team. His discharge of the multiple responsibilites of a modern correctional officer are described in Sector 4, Chapter 5 and Sector 5, Chapter 3 of Justice behind the Walls.

Mike Csoka, the Pacific region's Segregation Oversight Manager. Described in Justice behind the Walls as "the correctional man for all seasons", Mr. Csoka's many faceted roles as correctional supervisor at Matsqui and unit manager at Kent are described in several chapters of the book. This photograph was taken in 2002.

Mike Miller at Kent Institution in 2001.

Mr. Miller's experiences at Matsqui Institution in 1993 are described in Sector 2, Chapter 3 and Sector 3, Chapter 2 of Justice behind the Walls.

Robert Lamirand at Kent Institution in 2001.

Mr. Lamirand, like many federal prisoners has been state raised and has spent most of his teenage and adult life behind bars.
Roger Brock, the warden of Matsqui Institution from 1992-1999. Warden Brock's many faceted roles are the subject of Sector 2, Chapters 1-3 of Justice behind the Walls.
Stan Beacon, a correctional officer at Kent Institution with the view from Kent behind him. The picture was taken in 2001.

Stan Beacon, a correctional officer at Kent Institution, pointing to the statistical information he compiles as part of his responsibilities for maintaining the paper work for segregated prisoners.

His work is described in Sector 5, Chapter 5. His evidence in Hughie McDonald's trial is also described in Sector 2, Chapter 4 of Justice behind the Walls.

Wally Van Vugt, a correctional supervisor at Matsqui Instiuton in 2001.

The building behind him contains the segregation unit for which Mr. Van Vugt has supervisory responsibilities.
The travellers on the Red Road in 1993. From left to right: Cheam Nanaquetung, John Ironchild, Cory Bitternose, Rick Poirier, Jill Hummerstone, and Dennis Bigsky.
Cheam overlooking the Thompson River in the Fraser Canyon.
Cory at the confluence of the Thompson and Fraser rivers.
Looking down on First Nations fishing camps on the Fraser River.
Loading lava rock for use in the sweatlodge.
Rick Poirier, Cheam and Cory – definitely not an Alabama chain gang.
A law professor helps out. Legal training not required.
The four Brothers.
Preparing fire wood for lunch.
Gathering yarrow for use in Aboriginal ceremonies.
A field of sagebrush. Sage is an improtant part of Aboriginal ceremonies and the sage gathered on this occassion would be distributed to the guests at a powwow held at Matsqui Institution.
A cooling off at the end of a long day.
Along the Red Road the distinction between prisoner and guard dissolves in the fast flowing waters.
John Ironchild making a drum at Matsqui Institution.
Preparations for a sweatlodge ceremony at Matsqui Instution using the lava rock gathered the week before.
The sweatlodge at Matsqui with the main cell block in the background.
The powwow at Matsqui in August 1993.
The drummers' circle.
Dennis Bigsky and Cheam Nanaquetung at the powwow.
Dennis Bigsky, a respected and accomplished dancer.
Cory Bitternose, dancing in the shadow of razor wire.
Michael Jackson, with the drum given to him at the powwow by Cory Bitternose as a mark of mutual respect.
Rick Poirier, a respected traveller on the Red Road, with the drum given to him by the four Brothers for bridging the divide between the keeper and the kept.
